findings.title: Class named PlaceholderVlmProvider with 'Placeholder' in JSDoc comment
findings.description: The JSDoc comment says 'Placeholder VLM provider that returns a generic activity description.' and the class is named PlaceholderVlmProvider. Per CLAUDE.md forbidden patterns, PLACEHOLDER annotations are P0. The comment 'Will be replaced with a real AI SDK 6 integration in Task 8' on line 34 is a disguised TODO referencing a specific future task, which also violates the no-TODO rule.
findings.suggestion: Rename the class to something like StubVlmProvider or NoOpVlmProvider. Rewrite the JSDoc to describe what the class actually does ('Returns a generic activity description without calling any VLM API') rather than describing it as a placeholder. Remove the forward reference to Task 8.

findings.title: Intimacy state, lastTriggerTimes, and emotion actor are closure-scoped with no persistence
findings.description: The orchestrator holds three pieces of critical state in closure variables: `intimacy` (line 118), `lastTriggerTimes` (line 119), and `emotionActor` (line 117). All three are lost on app restart. Per CLAUDE.md rules: 'Business state in memory' is a forbidden pattern -- critical state must be persisted to DB/KV/Event Store. Intimacy score is a user-facing progression system (stranger -> acquaintance -> friend -> close_friend -> partner). Losing it on restart destroys user experience. The lastTriggerTimes map controls trigger cooldowns; losing it means duplicate triggers fire immediately after restart. The emotion state machine resets to 'idle', discarding the character's emotional continuity.
findings.suggestion: Persist intimacy score and stage to the existing DuckDB/SQLite store on each applyScoreChange call. Persist lastTriggerTimes as a JSON column or separate table. For the emotion actor, either persist the current state string and restore on startup via emotionActor.start() with initial state, or accept that emotion is ephemeral and document that decision explicitly. At minimum, add a TODO-free persistence interface (e.g., `AnimaOrchestratorDeps.persistence?: { loadState, saveState }`) and implement it against the app's existing storage.

findings.title: Old context-engine.ts and persona-engine.ts files are now dead code
findings.description: The orchestrator replaces setupContextEngine() and setupPersonaEngine() (confirmed by the index.ts diff removing their imports). However, the old files `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/context-engine.ts` and `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts` remain in the directory. They are no longer imported from any live entry point. This is orphan code -- per CLAUDE.md orphan detection rules, code not reachable from any entry point is dead-on-arrival.
findings.suggestion: Delete both files: `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/context-engine.ts` and `apps/stage-tamagotchi/src/main/services/anima/persona-engine.ts`. They have been fully superseded by `orchestrator.ts` and `setup-orchestrator.ts`. Keeping them creates confusion about which wiring path is active.

findings.title: DoNotDisturb module still not wired into the orchestrator
findings.description: The previous review (task-6) flagged that DoNotDisturb (canTrigger, recordTrigger, recordIgnore, recordUserInteraction) is exported from persona-engine but never consumed in the live wiring. This orchestrator was supposed to unify context-engine and persona-engine, but it still does not integrate DoNotDisturb. The handleProcessedContext function (line 122) calls evaluateTriggers without first checking canTrigger(), and does not call recordTrigger() after firing. The entire DoNotDisturb system (quiet hours, progressive backoff, ignore tracking, frequency limits) remains unreachable dead code in production despite having comprehensive unit tests.
findings.suggestion: In handleProcessedContext: (1) call canTrigger(dndState, ...) before evaluateTriggers, (2) call recordTrigger(dndState, now) after a trigger fires, (3) expose recordIgnore and recordUserInteraction in the AnimaOrchestrator interface. Add DND state to the orchestrator closure alongside intimacy and lastTriggerTimes.

findings.title: onError is optional -- when omitted, ScreenshotPipeline swallows errors silently and ActivityMonitor throws in setInterval
findings.description: AnimaOrchestratorConfig.onError is optional (line 56). The orchestrator passes it through to both ScreenshotPipeline and ActivityMonitor (lines 158, 166). In ScreenshotPipeline, when onError is undefined, this.onError?.(wrappedError) is a no-op -- the error is silently swallowed and tick() returns false (same value as a dedup skip). In ActivityMonitor, when onError is undefined and onContext throws, the error is re-thrown inside setInterval, which becomes an unhandled exception. The orchestrator provides no default error handling of its own.
findings.suggestion: Either make onError required in AnimaOrchestratorConfig, or provide a default handler inside createAnimaOrchestrator that at minimum logs errors via console.error (or a structured logger). This prevents both the silent-swallow path and the crash path. Consider: onError: config.onError ?? ((err) => { console.error('[AnimaOrchestrator] Unhandled error:', err) })

findings.title: handleProcessedContext has no try/catch; exceptions from persona-engine functions propagate unguarded
findings.description: handleProcessedContext (lines 122-147) calls evaluateTriggers, emotionActor.send, generateResponse, mapToAnimaEmotion, and config.onProactiveResponse without any try/catch. This function is registered as the onContext callback of ActivityMonitor. If any of these calls throw (e.g., evaluateTriggers receives malformed input, generateResponse encounters an unknown trigger, emotionActor is stopped), the exception propagates to ActivityMonitor.tick(). In the periodic setInterval path, ActivityMonitor.tick() wraps onContext in try/catch and routes to onError -- but only if onError is set. When onError is absent and start() is used, the error is re-thrown inside setInterval, causing an unhandled exception that crashes the Electron main process.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the body of handleProcessedContext in try/catch. Log the error with context (trigger input, emotion state) and forward to config.onError. This isolates persona-engine failures from crashing the aggregation loop.

findings.title: VLM provider failure path not tested in orchestrator
findings.description: The error handling section only tests screenshot capture failure (line 496-516). There is no test for when VlmProvider.describeImage() throws an error. The ScreenshotPipeline calls VLM after capture succeeds, so a VLM failure is a distinct failure mode that should be covered. The orchestrator wires onError for this path, but it is never verified with a failing VLM.
findings.suggestion: Add a test with a VlmProvider that throws (e.g., 'API rate limit exceeded') and verify that onError is called with the appropriate wrapped error message. Example: create a FailingVlmProvider class with '// Test Double rationale: VlmProvider is an external LLM API' and verify the error propagates correctly through tickScreenshot().

findings.title: Missing error test for handleProcessedContext failures (trigger evaluation, response generation)
findings.description: The error handling test suite (lines 494-516) only covers screenshot provider failures. There is no test for errors thrown by handleProcessedContext (the onContext callback of ActivityMonitor). This means there is no verification that errors from evaluateTriggers, generateResponse, or onProactiveResponse are correctly caught, wrapped, and forwarded to onError. Given that handleProcessedContext has no try/catch (see errors-001), this gap means the crash path is both untested and unhandled.
findings.suggestion: Add test cases: (1) onProactiveResponse callback that throws -- verify error reaches onError, (2) simulate an error in the trigger evaluation path by providing malformed data, (3) test behavior when onError is not provided (verify no silent swallowing and no crash in tick path).

findings.title: setup-orchestrator onError handler only logs -- does not escalate or recover
findings.description: The production wiring in setupAnimaOrchestrator sets onError to (error) => { log.withError(error).error('Anima orchestrator error') } (lines 69-71). This logs the error but does not escalate it, stop the orchestrator, emit a metric, or take any recovery action. Repeated failures (e.g., screen capture permissions revoked) will log indefinitely without any circuit-breaking or alerting. This is effectively catch-and-log-only, which CLAUDE.md explicitly forbids under error_handling rules: 'catch (e) { console.log(e) }' -- Logging without handling.
findings.suggestion: Add meaningful error handling: count consecutive errors and stop the pipeline after N failures (circuit breaker), emit an error metric/event for observability, or at minimum re-throw for the DI container to handle. Example: track errorCount, and after 5 consecutive failures, call orchestrator.stop() and log a FATAL-level message.

findings.title: createAnimaOrchestrator function is 100 lines long
findings.description: The createAnimaOrchestrator function spans lines 113-213 (100 lines). While internally well-structured with named inner functions, the function body as a whole crosses the 100-line threshold. The function contains: (1) state initialization (lines 117-121), (2) handleProcessedContext inner function (lines 122-147), (3) handleScreenshotContext inner function (lines 149-151), (4) pipeline/monitor construction (lines 153-167), and (5) the returned interface object (lines 169-212). The returned object alone is ~43 lines of method definitions.
findings.suggestion: Extract the returned object's method implementations as standalone inner functions. Alternatively, since the function is logically well-partitioned already (inner functions are short and clear), this is borderline acceptable. Consider extracting only if the function grows further.

findings.title: PlaceholderVlmProvider returns hardcoded values in production path
findings.description: PlaceholderVlmProvider always returns { description: 'User is working at their computer', entities: [], activity: 'working' }. It is instantiated unconditionally in setupAnimaOrchestrator() (line 55) with no configuration mechanism to swap in a real VLM provider. While the comment says 'Will be replaced with a real AI SDK 6 integration in Task 8', the class is wired into the production DI graph via injeca.provide. The pipeline will run every 60 seconds, waste CPU on screenshot capture + pHash computation, then discard all visual understanding by returning a static string. The log.warn on every call will flood logs at ~1 per minute.
findings.suggestion: Either: (1) Accept a vlmProvider via config/env and throw if not configured (fail-fast), or (2) skip the screenshot pipeline entirely when no real VLM is configured (add an `enabled` flag to AnimaOrchestratorConfig), or (3) make the PlaceholderVlmProvider return an explicit 'no VLM configured' sentinel that downstream code handles by skipping VLM-dependent triggers. Also reduce the log.warn to log at most once (not per tick).

findings.title: No ActivityEvent source wired to the orchestrator
findings.description: setupAnimaOrchestrator creates and starts the orchestrator but never calls recordActivity(). The orchestrator exposes recordActivity() in its public interface, but no event source (Electron powerMonitor, window focus tracking, keyboard/mouse activity, IPC from renderer) is connected. Without activity events, the ActivityMonitor's sliding window will always be empty, continuousWorkDurationMs will be 0, and time-based triggers (rest-reminder, late-night-work) can never fire. The orchestrator starts running but produces no useful output because it has no input.
findings.suggestion: Wire at least one ActivityEvent source. Options: (1) use Electron's powerMonitor for idle detection, (2) use the existing rdev crate for keyboard/mouse activity, (3) accept activity events via IPC from the renderer process, or (4) generate synthetic events from the screenshot pipeline's VLM output (which already includes app name and activity type).

findings.title: buildTriggerInput hardcodes several fields to safe defaults, limiting trigger coverage
findings.description: buildTriggerInput (line 222-247) defaults isFirstActivityToday=false, matchedImportantDate=false, hasNearDeadlineTodos=false, windowSwitchesInLast5Min=0, and previousFocusDurationMs=0. This means 6 of the 11 ALL_TRIGGERS can never fire: T01 (morning-greeting needs isFirstActivityToday), T04 (entertainment-switch needs previousAppCategory), T07 (important-date needs matchedImportantDate), T08 (task-due needs hasNearDeadlineTodos), T09 (high-frequency-switch needs windowSwitchesInLast5Min), T11 (return-to-desktop needs timeSinceLastActivityMs from a different source). Only T03 (rest-reminder) and potentially T05 (late-night-work) and T06 (evening-summary) are functional. The TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP (line 63) defines mappings for all 11 triggers, creating a false impression of complete coverage.
findings.suggestion: Document which triggers are functional now vs. require external data sources. Consider removing non-functional triggers from ALL_TRIGGERS in the orchestrator config to avoid false evaluations. Track the missing data sources (activity session tracking, calendar integration, todo system, window focus tracker) as concrete next steps.

findings.title: TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP uses plain `string` key instead of trigger name literal union
findings.description: TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP is typed as `Record<string, EmotionEvent>`, which accepts any string key. The trigger names are a closed set defined in ALL_TRIGGERS (e.g., 'morning-greeting', 'rest-reminder'). A plain `string` key means: (1) typos in keys are undetectable at compile time, (2) looking up a trigger name that has no mapping silently returns `undefined`. This is a case of primitive obsession on a domain concept.
findings.suggestion: Extract a `TriggerName` literal union from ALL_TRIGGERS (e.g., `type TriggerName = typeof ALL_TRIGGERS[number]['name']`) and type the map as `Partial<Record<TriggerName, EmotionEvent>>` or a full `Record<TriggerName, EmotionEvent>` if every trigger must have a mapping. This catches typos at compile time and makes the closed set explicit.

findings.title: Near-duplicate PNG buffer generation blocks in test setup
findings.description: Lines 20-32 and 34-46 contain near-identical PNG generation code. The only difference is the conditional: `x < width / 2` vs `y < height / 2`. This same pattern also appears in packages/context-engine/src/__tests__/phash.test.ts (lines 67, 80). The duplication is 13 lines per block.
findings.suggestion: Extract a generateTestPng(width, height, splitFn) helper function. The splitFn parameter makes the split-direction difference explicit. This helper could also be shared with phash.test.ts via a test utility module.

findings.title: Heavily duplicated test orchestrator setup across 11 test cases
findings.description: The pattern of creating SequentialScreenshotProvider, StubVlmProvider, proactiveEvents array, and calling createAnimaOrchestrator with nearly identical config appears 11 times. Nine tests use aggregationIntervalMs: 1000 and eight use initialIntimacyScore: 20. Each test also calls orchestrator.stop() in its body rather than in afterEach. This is approximately 8-12 lines of boilerplate per test.
findings.suggestion: Extract a createTestOrchestrator(opts) factory function that returns { orchestrator, proactiveEvents, screenshotProvider, vlmProvider }. Each test then calls this factory with only the options that differ. Also consider moving orchestrator.stop() to afterEach.

findings.title: onProactiveResponse only logs -- proactive responses never reach the UI
findings.description: The setupAnimaOrchestrator wires onProactiveResponse to call log.info (lines 62-67). This means when a trigger fires successfully (e.g., rest-reminder after 2 hours of work), the response message is logged to the console but never sent to the renderer process via IPC or Eventa. The UI layer has no way to display proactive messages from the Anima pipeline. This makes the entire end-to-end pipeline produce observable side effects only in the main process log -- it is a horizontal-only integration that does not deliver value to the user.
findings.suggestion: Wire onProactiveResponse to emit an IPC event (via Eventa or ipcMain.emit) to the renderer, so the UI can display the proactive message. Define the IPC contract in the shared directory first, then implement both the emit (main) and receive (renderer) sides.

findings.title: Trigger cooldown behavior not tested at orchestrator level
findings.description: The orchestrator maintains lastTriggerTimes (orchestrator.ts line 119) and passes it to evaluateTriggers. However, no test verifies that the same trigger does not fire again within its cooldown period when tickActivity() is called multiple times. The cooldown logic is tested at the persona-engine unit level, but the integration wiring through the orchestrator (which manages the mutable lastTriggerTimes state) is not verified.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that calls tickActivity() twice in succession after 2+ hours of work. The first call should fire T03, the second call (within cooldown) should not fire any trigger. Then advance time past cooldown and verify T03 fires again.

findings.title: AnimaOrchestratorConfig lacks readonly modifiers on all fields
findings.description: AnimaOrchestratorConfig has optional fields with sensible defaults (good), but none of the fields are marked `readonly`. Since this is a configuration object passed into the factory function, it should be immutable after construction. The persona-engine types already use `readonly` consistently.
findings.suggestion: Add `readonly` modifier to all fields of AnimaOrchestratorConfig and AnimaOrchestratorDeps, matching the convention established in persona-engine types.

findings.title: TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP lookup silently skips unknown trigger names
findings.description: When a trigger fires, the orchestrator looks up the trigger name in TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP (line 133). If the trigger name is not in the map, emotionEvent is undefined, and the if(emotionEvent) check on line 134 silently skips the emotion transition. This means a new trigger added to ALL_TRIGGERS without a corresponding entry in TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP will fire its response but with a stale/incorrect emotion state. No warning is logged for unmapped triggers.
findings.suggestion: Log a warning when a triggered name has no entry in TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP. Alternatively, use a compile-time exhaustive check by typing TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP as Record<TriggerName, EmotionEvent>.

findings.title: Unsafe type assertion: `emotionActor.getSnapshot().value as PersonaEmotion`
findings.description: The xstate actor snapshot value is cast to `PersonaEmotion` with `as` on lines 138 and 194. This bypasses type safety -- if the emotion state machine's states ever diverge from the PersonaEmotion union, this cast will silently produce incorrect values. The `as` assertion is used in two places, creating a maintenance hazard.
findings.suggestion: Either: (1) Type the xstate machine output so `getSnapshot().value` already returns `PersonaEmotion` without a cast (preferred), or (2) add a runtime guard. Avoid duplicating the `as` cast -- extract a helper like `getCurrentEmotion(actor): PersonaEmotion`.

findings.title: No test for empty activity events edge case
findings.description: No test verifies what happens when tickActivity() is called without any prior recordActivity() calls. The orchestrator should produce a ProcessedContext with safe defaults (zero duration, empty recentApps, no triggers fire). This is a boundary condition for application startup where the orchestrator starts before any activity events arrive.
findings.suggestion: Add a test: create orchestrator, immediately call tickActivity() without any recordActivity() calls. Assert that context.activity.continuousWorkDurationMs === 0, context.activity.recentApps is empty, and no proactive events fire.

findings.title: Unsafe type assertion on emotion actor snapshot value
findings.description: emotionActor.getSnapshot().value is cast as PersonaEmotion (lines 138 and 194) without runtime validation. If the xstate actor is in a stopped/errored state, or if the machine definition changes and introduces nested states, .value could be an object instead of a string, or undefined. The unsafe cast silently produces incorrect data that propagates to generateResponse and the UI layer.
findings.suggestion: Add a runtime guard: validate that the snapshot value is one of the known PersonaEmotion string literals before using it. Throw a typed error or fall back to 'idle' with a warning log if the value is unexpected.

findings.title: No test for unmapped trigger name in TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP
findings.description: When a trigger fires with a name not present in TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP (line 63-75), the emotionEvent lookup returns undefined and the emotion state machine is not updated (line 134 guards with 'if (emotionEvent)'). However, the response is still generated with the old emotion state. No test verifies this graceful degradation path.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that uses a custom TriggerCondition with a name not in TRIGGER_EMOTION_MAP. Verify that the trigger still generates a response (via onProactiveResponse) but the emotion state remains unchanged.

findings.title: buildTriggerInput hardcoded defaults not tested for correctness
findings.description: The private buildTriggerInput function (lines 222-247) hardcodes several default values: isFirstActivityToday=false, previousAppCategory='work', matchedImportantDate=false, hasNearDeadlineTodos=false, windowSwitchesInLast5Min=0, previousFocusDurationMs=0. These defaults affect which triggers can fire. No test verifies that these defaults do not accidentally enable triggers that should not fire with real data.
findings.suggestion: Add a test that creates an orchestrator with ALL_TRIGGERS enabled, provides activity data at various times of day, and asserts that only the expected triggers fire given the hardcoded defaults.

findings.title: Screenshot deduplication behavior not tested at orchestrator level
findings.description: The ScreenshotPipeline internally uses pHash deduplication (same screenshot not re-processed). The test creates SequentialScreenshotProvider with distinct images (pngA, pngB) but never tests calling tickScreenshot() twice with the same image to verify the orchestrator properly handles the 'no change' case (pipeline returns false).
findings.suggestion: Add a test: call tickScreenshot() twice with the same image (SequentialScreenshotProvider([pngA, pngA])). Verify the second call returns false and the VLM provider is not called a second time.

findings.title: setupAnimaOrchestrator has no error handling for constructor-phase failures
findings.description: setupAnimaOrchestrator() (lines 53-80) instantiates ElectronScreenshotProvider, PlaceholderVlmProvider, calls createAnimaOrchestrator, and then calls orchestrator.start() -- all without try/catch. If createEmotionActor() fails to initialize, or if orchestrator.start() triggers an immediate error, the exception propagates to injeca.provide() and may leave partially-constructed resources.
findings.suggestion: Wrap the setup in try/catch. On failure, log with context and either re-throw a typed error for injeca to handle, or return a no-op orchestrator that surfaces its construction failure.

findings.title: No error handling around emotionActor.stop() in stop() method
findings.description: The stop() method (lines 175-179) calls pipeline.stop(), monitor.stop(), and emotionActor.stop() sequentially. If emotionActor.stop() throws, pipeline and monitor have already stopped but the error propagates unhandled. There is no try/catch and no guarantee of cleanup atomicity.
findings.suggestion: Wrap each stop call in its own try/catch, or use a finally-chain pattern to ensure all three components are stopped regardless of individual failures.

findings.title: ScreenshotPipeline.start() has async tick in setInterval without concurrency guard propagated to orchestrator
findings.description: While ScreenshotPipeline internally has a `ticking` guard, the ActivityMonitor.start() invocation also uses setInterval. If ActivityMonitor.tick() calls handleProcessedContext which modifies `intimacy` and `lastTriggerTimes` (closure state), and the activity aggregation interval is shorter than the trigger evaluation time, concurrent ticks could read stale lastTriggerTimes and fire duplicate triggers.
findings.suggestion: Verify that ActivityMonitor has a similar ticking guard. Alternatively, since this runs in Node.js single-threaded event loop, the risk is lower if all operations are synchronous within a tick -- document the concurrency assumption explicitly.

findings.title: setup-orchestrator.ts has no corresponding test file
findings.description: setup-orchestrator.ts is a new imperative shell file that wires the ElectronScreenshotProvider and PlaceholderVlmProvider to the orchestrator. It has no test file. The wiring logic (config assembly, onProactiveResponse callback, onError callback, orchestrator.start() call) could be tested by extracting the config-building logic.
findings.suggestion: This file is an imperative shell wiring module. Consider extracting the config-building logic into a testable pure function, or document that this file is covered by the orchestrator.test.ts integration tests for the core pipeline.

findings.title: Forward-looking comment referencing 'Task 8' will rot
findings.description: The comment 'Will be replaced with a real AI SDK 6 integration in Task 8' references a specific future task and technology. This is a disguised TODO that will become stale.
findings.suggestion: Remove the forward reference entirely. If the intent needs to be documented, file a tracked issue and reference it by issue number.

findings.title: Forward-looking comment 'will be enhanced when respective systems are implemented'
findings.description: The comment 'These will be enhanced when the respective systems are implemented' on buildTriggerInput is a disguised TODO. It describes future intent without any concrete tracking reference.
findings.suggestion: Replace with a factual description of the current state: 'Fields that require external sources (memory, todo system) use safe default values.' Drop the forward-looking sentence entirely.

findings.title: Type assertion 'as PersonaEmotion' used repeatedly without runtime guard
findings.description: Lines 138 and 194 cast emotionActor.getSnapshot().value as PersonaEmotion. If the xstate machine definition changes and adds nested states or parallel regions, the snapshot value structure changes from a simple string to an object, and this cast would silently produce incorrect values.
findings.suggestion: Create a helper function in persona-engine that safely extracts the current emotion from the actor snapshot, with a runtime check that the value is a valid PersonaEmotion string.

findings.title: PlaceholderVlmProvider in production code is a TODO pattern
findings.description: PlaceholderVlmProvider (line 36-45) returns hardcoded generic results and has a comment 'Will be replaced with a real AI SDK 6 integration in Task 8.' This is effectively a TODO/placeholder in production code.
findings.suggestion: Consider adding a configuration check that warns at startup if no real VLM provider is configured, rather than warning on every describeImage() call.

findings.title: Magic time expressions could use named constants
findings.description: Lines 162 and 164 use inline time expressions `15 * 60 * 1000` (15 minutes) and `3 * 60 * 60 * 1000` (3 hours) as default values. The test file already defines named constants for similar values.
findings.suggestion: Extract named constants: DEFAULT_AGGREGATION_INTERVAL_MS, DEFAULT_EVENT_RETENTION_MS, DEFAULT_SCREENSHOT_INTERVAL_MS.

findings.title: Misplaced 'Test Double rationale: N/A' comment on production class
findings.description: The comment 'Test Double rationale: N/A -- this is the real implementation for Electron.' is placed on ElectronScreenshotProvider, which is the real production implementation. The annotation pattern is meant for test doubles, not production classes.
findings.suggestion: Remove the 'Test Double rationale: N/A' line. The JSDoc already describes the class purpose.

findings.title: Test Double rationale comment in production code is misleading
findings.description: Line 14 contains 'Test Double rationale: N/A -- this is the real implementation for Electron.' on the ElectronScreenshotProvider class. The pattern is meant for test files to justify test doubles, not for production classes.
findings.suggestion: Remove the 'Test Double rationale: N/A' comment from the production class.

findings.title: AnimaProactiveEvent.timestamp is bare `number` -- primitive obsession for timestamps
findings.description: AnimaProactiveEvent.timestamp: number does not distinguish epoch-ms from epoch-s or other numeric values. This is a recurring pattern in the codebase.
findings.suggestion: Consider a branded type like `type EpochMs = number & { readonly __brand: 'EpochMs' }` for timestamps. This is a codebase-wide concern.

findings.title: Repeated continuous-work test pattern could share a helper
findings.description: At least 6 tests follow the exact pattern: (1) set system time, (2) call makeContinuousWorkEvents, (3) loop to recordActivity, (4) call tickActivity. This 7-line block is repeated nearly verbatim.
findings.suggestion: Extract a simulateContinuousWork(orchestrator, durationMs, overrides?) helper function that encapsulates the pattern.

findings.title: Redundant comment 'Ensure the last event is at endTime' restates obvious code
findings.description: The comment directly restates the if-condition and push statement that follows it. The code is self-documenting.
findings.suggestion: Remove the comment. The code is clear enough without it.

findings.title: Runtime warning message in PlaceholderVlmProvider references 'placeholder'
findings.description: The log.warn message includes the word 'placeholder' which echoes the forbidden pattern and will appear in production logs if the class is never replaced.
findings.suggestion: Rephrase to 'VLM provider not configured: returning generic activity description. Set up a VLM provider for AI-powered screen analysis.'

findings.title: AnimaOrchestratorConfig has 7 fields mixing concerns
findings.description: AnimaOrchestratorConfig has 7 fields spanning three concerns: timing, persona state, and callbacks. Currently manageable but could become unwieldy.
findings.suggestion: No code change needed now. If this config object grows beyond 10 fields, consider grouping into sub-objects (timing, persona, callbacks).

findings.title: AnimaOrchestrator interface is well-designed with clear behavioral contract
findings.description: The AnimaOrchestrator interface defines the behavioral contract without exposing implementation details. The factory function createAnimaOrchestrator returns this interface, hiding internal emotionActor, intimacy state, pipeline, and monitor. The tickScreenshot() and tickActivity() methods allow deterministic testing without timers. This follows the Functional Core / Imperative Shell architecture correctly.
findings.suggestion: No action required. This is a well-encapsulated design.

positive_observations: AnimaOrchestrator interface is well-designed with clear behavioral contract -- factory function hides implementation details while exposing deterministic tick methods for testing
buildTriggerInput correctly bridges context-engine and persona-engine domains as an anti-corruption layer, keeping the two domains independent
Orchestrator test file has comprehensive coverage with 11 test cases covering the main pipeline paths, proactive trigger scenarios, emotion state transitions, and intimacy progression
The overall Functional Core / Imperative Shell separation is clean: persona-engine functions remain pure, orchestrator handles IO composition
xstate emotion machine integration provides a principled approach to emotion state transitions rather than ad-hoc flag management
ScreenshotPipeline uses pHash deduplication to avoid redundant VLM calls -- a thoughtful performance optimization
The new orchestrator successfully unifies context-engine and persona-engine into a single coherent pipeline, simplifying the previous two-file setup
